title: 💰 How to Prepare for an IRS Audit Step by Step
description:
How to actually prepare if you receive an IRS audit notice in 2026. Real timelines, real documentation, and the response strategy that keeps adjustments to a minimum. Plus the UK HMRC compliance check process.

Personal finance, IRS audit preparation, audit defense, HMRC compliance check, freelancer audit 2026.

segments.voice: Getting an IRS audit notice in the mail makes most people panic. The actual audit rate is statistically tiny, around zero point three eight percent for individual returns per the IRS Data Book two thousand twenty-three, but if you are the one selected, the panic feels real. Today, the step-by-step preparation that minimizes adjustments and keeps the process under control.

Step zero, do not ignore the letter. The IRS gives you thirty days to respond unless the letter says otherwise. Ignoring it triggers default adjustments where the IRS assesses every disallowed deduction without your input. The collection process that follows is much harder to fight than the audit itself. Open the letter, read it carefully, mark the deadline.

Read the notice type carefully. Different IRS notices mean different things. CP2000 is an automated underreporter notice, usually a 1099 mismatch, often resolvable by mail. Letter 525 indicates a correspondence audit, mail-based, focused on specific items. Letter 2205 indicates a field audit with an in-person meeting. Each requires a different preparation strategy.

Step one, identify the specific issues. The notice lists exactly which line items the IRS is questioning. Common audit triggers include large home office deductions, vehicle deductions without supporting logs, business meals at high percentages, unusual charitable contributions, and Schedule C losses claimed three years running. Knowing the issue tells you what documentation to assemble.

Step two, gather the documentation. For each questioned line item, pull every relevant document. Bank statements showing the deposits and payments, receipts and invoices, mileage logs, contracts, bills, anything that supports the position you took on the return. Digital files in cloud storage make this dramatically easier than reconstructing paper records under deadline pressure.

Step three, organize the response. Create a single master folder for the audit, with subfolders by issue. Inside each issue folder, organize chronologically by date. Add a one-page summary at the top of each folder explaining what the documents prove and how they support the deduction. The IRS auditor will appreciate the organization, and clarity helps you mentally.

Step four, reconcile to the return. For each questioned item, build a reconciliation that shows how the documents support the line on your return. Total of receipts equals deduction claimed. Total mileage from log times rate equals vehicle deduction. The reconciliation makes the auditor's job easier and signals professional preparation, which often softens the overall tone of the audit.

Step five, decide on representation. You can represent yourself, but for any audit involving over five thousand dollars in proposed adjustments, hiring an enrolled agent or CPA is usually worth it. They typically charge two hundred to four hundred an hour. The fee usually saves multiples in adjustments avoided. Form 2848 Power of Attorney lets them deal directly with the IRS on your behalf.

What an enrolled agent versus CPA brings. Enrolled agents are tax-only specialists licensed by the Treasury Department. CPAs handle broader accounting work including audits and financial statements. For pure tax representation, an EA experienced in audits is often the most cost-effective choice. The National Association of Enrolled Agents has a directory at naea.org.

Step six, write the response letter. Even with representation, you should review the response letter before submission. The letter should restate the audit issues, explain the legal basis for each deduction with citations, summarize the supporting documentation, and request the audit close without adjustment. Be polite, factual, never emotional. The auditor reads dozens of these.

Step seven, the meeting if applicable. Field audits include an in-person meeting at the IRS office or your business location. Bring all original documents, organized as we discussed. Answer only the question asked. Do not volunteer additional information that opens new issues. Take notes during the meeting on what was discussed and what additional documents the auditor wants.
segments.visual: audit meeting in person IRS office

segments.voice: What never to do during an audit. Never lie or misrepresent facts, the penalties for false statements escalate dramatically. Never destroy documents that exist, even if you wish they did not. Never pay the proposed adjustment immediately without reviewing whether it is correct. Never ignore correspondence at any stage of the process.

Step eight, review the proposed changes. After the audit, the IRS issues an examination report or Form 4549 listing proposed adjustments. Read it line by line. Compare each adjustment to the position you took on the return and the documentation provided. The auditor sometimes makes mistakes that are easily corrected at this stage.

Step nine, negotiate or appeal. If you disagree with the proposed adjustments, you have options. First, request a manager conference, often resolves issues without formal appeal. Second, appeal to the IRS Office of Appeals, which is structurally independent and often settles cases at fifty cents on the dollar. Third, petition Tax Court, generally a last resort.

The Office of Appeals advantage. Per IRS Restructuring and Reform Act of nineteen ninety-eight, Appeals is independent of the audit division. Appeals officers consider hazards of litigation, meaning they think about how a case would actually play out in court. Many cases settle at Appeals because both sides recognize the cost and uncertainty of going further.

Tax Court basics. If you petition Tax Court within ninety days of the statutory notice of deficiency, you do not have to pay the disputed tax first. Most other federal courts require pre-payment then suing for refund. Tax Court has a small case division for disputes under fifty thousand dollars where pro se representation is more practical.

What happens if you owe. If the audit results in tax owed, you have options. Pay in full, the simplest. Set up an installment agreement if you owe under fifty thousand, online streamlined process at irs.gov. Submit an offer in compromise if your financial situation is dire and full payment is impossible, Form 656 with 433-A financial disclosure.

Penalty considerations. Audits often add accuracy-related penalties at twenty percent of the underpayment under IRC section 6662. The IRS waives these for reasonable cause, like reliance on a tax professional or honest mistake of fact. The penalty abatement letter requesting reasonable cause should be drafted carefully and may save you the entire penalty amount.

The statute of limitations safety net. The IRS generally has three years from filing to assess additional tax. Six years if there is substantial under-reporting of twenty-five percent or more. Unlimited if you filed a fraudulent return or never filed at all. So old tax years eventually fall off the audit radar, except in cases of significant misreporting.

Common audit triggers to be aware of. Schedule C with consistent losses across multiple years, claiming round numbers like ten thousand exactly for every category, very high deductions relative to income, claiming a hobby as a business under IRC section 183 hobby loss rules, missing 1099 income that the IRS already has from third parties.

UK readers, the HMRC compliance check process. HMRC opens compliance checks under the Taxes Management Act 1970 section 9A for individuals or schedule eighteen of the Finance Act 1998 for companies. The opening letter explains what is being checked and the deadline to respond. The HMRC charter governs how officers should conduct themselves throughout.

HMRC compliance check timing. A typical compliance check takes three to twelve months. Initial requests for information are usually given thirty days. Field visits to your premises are scheduled in advance. The HMRC officer can request specific records, ask about transactions, and propose adjustments. The First-Tier Tax Tribunal handles disputes that do not resolve through internal review.

Practical post-audit habits. After surviving any audit, tighten your record keeping going forward. Cloud storage with automatic categorization, mileage tracking apps with GPS logs, expense categorization software, separate business and personal accounts. Each of these makes future audits dramatically easier. Many freelancers come out of an audit better organized than ever.
